coredata.dc:title: A “performance-inheritance” strategy for achieving hydrophilic/hydrophobic fluorescent biomass-derived carbon dots through simple solvothermal treatments
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Fu
He, Pinyi
Wang, Xinyu
Yang, Guohui
Yu, Xu
Yao, Yongkang
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: It remains a challenge to obtain hydrophilic/hydrophobic materials with promising biocompatibility and environmental compatibility by simple methods. Herein, we designed a “performance-inheritance” strategy and for the first time constructed hydrophilic/hydrophobic fluorescent spirulina-derived carbon dots (CDs) through simple solvothermal treatments. The contact angle of hydrophobic CDs with near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence is 110°, while hydrophilic CDs with blue fluorescence is 19° We further revealed that the NIR luminescence is derived from the chlorophyll structure, while blue fluorescence is from the surface states. Based on the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of CDs, a ratiometric sensor was constructed to detect water content in organic solutions. In addition, CDs with NIR emission have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, making them efficient probes for bioimaging. This strategy provides a simple method for the high value-added conversion of algae and a new perspective for the “design-preparation-application” of biomass-derived CDs with specific properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Hydrophilicity-hydrophobicity
Sustainable chemistry
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of different spirulina powder extracts
Determination of water content in organic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption, FL excitation, and FL emission spectra of (a) spirulina ethanol solution, (d) s-RCDs, and (g) s-BCDs, respectively, and the insets are photographs of the corresponding solutions under daylight and 365 nm UV light. FL spectra of (b) spirulina ethanol solution, (e) s-RCDs, and (h) s-BCDs at different excitation wavelengths. 2D fluorescence topographical maps of (c) spirulina ethanol solution, (f) s-RCDs, and (i) s-BCDs.

(a) TEM images of s-RCDs (the top inset is the HRTEM image, and the bottom right inset is the diameter distribution histogram). (b) TEM image of s-BCDs and the inset photograph of corresponding dots diameter distribution histogram. (c-f) XRD patterns, Raman spectra, XPS scan spectra, and FT-IR spectra of s-RCDs (red line) and s-BCDs (blue line).

(a-c) UV–vis absorption spectra of spirulina aqueous extract, phycocyanin aqueous solution, and phycocyanin after hydrothermal treatment at 100 °C for 3 h (d-f) FL emission spectra of spirulina aqueous extract, phycocyanin aqueous solution, and phycocyanin after hydrothermal treatment at 100 °C for 3 h And inset photograph of the structural formula for phycocyanin.
Proposed possible formation process and schematic luminescence mechanism of s-RCDs and s-BCDs.

(a) Fluorograms of s-RCDs ethanol solution and s-BCDs aqueous solution with equal amounts of (I) water, (II) ethanol, (III) n-butanol, (IV) N,N-dimethylformamide, (V) cyclohexane, (VI) toluene, (VII) dichloromethane, and (VIII) chloroform after shaking and standing, respectively. (b) FL emission spectra of s-RCDs and s-BCDs solution with different water content of 1,4-dioxane. (c) The relationship between FL intensity ratio of 435 nm and 670 nm (I435/I670) and different water content in 1,4-dioxane.
Laser confocal fluorescent images of cells incubated with s-RCDs.

head.ce:title: A “performance-inheritance” strategy for achieving hydrophilic/hydrophobic fluorescent biomass-derived carbon dots through simple solvothermal treatments

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It remains a challenge to obtain hydrophilic/hydrophobic materials with promising biocompatibility and environmental compatibility by simple methods. Herein, we designed a “performance-inheritance” strategy and for the first time constructed hydrophilic/hydrophobic fluorescent spirulina-derived carbon dots (CDs) through simple solvothermal treatments. The contact angle of hydrophobic CDs with near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence is 110°, while hydrophilic CDs with blue fluorescence is 19° We further revealed that the NIR luminescence is derived from the chlorophyll structure, while blue fluorescence is from the surface states. Based on the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of CDs, a ratiometric sensor was constructed to detect water content in organic solutions. In addition, CDs with NIR emission have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, making them efficient probes for bioimaging. This strategy provides a simple method for the high value-added conversion of algae and a new perspective for the “design-preparation-application” of biomass-derived CDs with specific properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of materials refers to the characteristics of the surface in contact with liquid, which plays a crucial role in different application fields. Hydrophobicity means that the surface of the material does not have an affinity for water or other polar solvents, which has been considered one of the key factors for the therapeutic ability of drugs, and thus has a wide range of needs in the biomedical field [1-3]. Hydrophilicity, on the other hand, defines a surface that has an affinity for water and can be wetted by water, which has a broad application prospect in the fields of sensing detection, the construction industry, and textiles [4-6].

Generally, surface modification is the main way to adjust the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of materials: (i) physical modification. It is mainly coated with hydrophilic/hydrophobic monomers or polymers on the surface of the materials [7,8]. (ii) chemical modification. It is mainly through various chemical methods to graft hydrophilic/hydrophobic groups or polymers on the surface [9-11]. However, such synthetic methods usually require organic or inorganic reagents, as well as cumbersome synthesis and complex post-processing. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a simple method to regulate the hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties of materials with biological and environmental compatibility.

Carbon dots, especially biomass-derived carbon dots, as an emerging carbon nanomaterial with great application potential, have good biological and environmental compatibility, excellent optical properties, easy functionalization, and anti-photo-bleaching properties [12-16]. To date, although there have been some reports of hydrophilic/hydrophobic CDs through modifying hydrophilic/hydrophobic groups or polymers on the surface [17-20], it is still necessary to simplify the synthesis steps and prepare environmentally friendly CDs. In particular, biomass has diverse structures and complex compositions, so relatively little research has been done on the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of biomass-derived CDs.

Among many biomass, the genus spirulina, as photosynthetic cyanobacteria, accounts for more than 30 % of the world's biomass production because of its high protein concentration, chlorophyll, and phycocyanin content [21,22]. First, spirulina is a renewable, low-cost biomass that can be used to manufacture a variety of value-added products [23,24]. Therefore, the preparation of CDs from spirulina as biomass precursors is widely available, easy to obtain, and can be prepared industrially. Second, spirulina is photoautotrophic with simple biological requirements and is one of the fastest-growing photosynthetic organisms, making it cheap to cultivate and proliferate [25]. It is worth noting that the structure of spirulina is relatively simple, and is rich in chlorophyll as a hydrophobic substance (Spirulina may contain about 2 wt% of chlorophyll, which is 10 times more than that found in ordinary terrestrial plants [26].) and unique phycocyanin with hydrophilicity that can account for up to 20 % of spirulina (dry basis) [27]. According to the previous work of our research group, the deep red fluorescence (FL) of leaf-derived CDs and moss-based CDs is closely related to the structure of chlorophyll, and chlorophyll is hydrophobic, so the CDs derived from it should also be hydrophobic, which attracts us to further expand our understanding of the luminescence source of CDs and explore the influence of the different compositions in the biomass on the performance of CDs [28,29]. And there is sufficient evidence to show that precursor fragments on the surface of CDs can be retained without decomposition reaction at a suitable pyrolysis temperature, which is expected to preserve the properties of precursor molecules [30-32]. We imagine whether it is possible to use spirulina as a precursor, extract precursor components with different properties in different solvents, and finally obtain fluorescent CDs that retain the characteristics of precursor components after a certain reaction (Fig. 1 ).

Based on the above considerations, we designed a “performance-inheritance” strategy. The hydrophobic components (mainly chlorophyll) and hydrophilic components (mainly phycocyanin) in spirulina were extracted with organic solvents and water, respectively. Through a one-step solvothermal reaction, red fluorescent hydrophobic CDs and blue fluorescent hydrophilic CDs inherited the characteristics of the precursor components were obtained. Combined with the structure of the precursor components, the luminescence mechanism of red and blue fluorescence was proposed. In view of the hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity of CDs, a ratiometric sensor is designed to detect water content in organic solutions. In addition, CDs with excellent near-infrared emission show great application prospects in the field of cell imaging. This work lays a good foundation for the green and simple synthesis of CDs with specific properties, which will greatly stimulate the large-scale preparation and application of CDs.

Spirulina powder was purchased from New Daze Co., Ltd. (Fuqing, China). Phycocyanin was purchased from Jiangsu Aikang Biomedical R&D Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Toluene (Tol), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone (DMK), cyclohexane (CYH), dichloromethane (DCM), chloroform (TCM), n-butanol (NBA), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1,4-dioxane and anhydrous ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (18.1 MΩ cm) produced from a Millipore Direct-Q system was used throughout the experiments. Unless otherwise specified, all reagents are analytical reagents (AR) and can be used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different spirulina powder extracts
ce:section.ce:para: Different extracts were prepared by adding 0.02 g spirulina powder to 8 mL anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone (DMK), toluene (Tol), dichloromethane (DCM), cyclohexane (CYH) and water (H2O), respectively. In particular, water extract was ultrasound for 10 min. All extracts were shielded from light for 24 h and subsequently filtered through syringe filters (pore size 0.22 μm) to remove solid particles and obtain spirulina organic and aqueous extracts for the following test.

The red/blue fluorescent spirulina-based carbon dots were prepared by a simple and green solvothermal method. First of all, combined with our previous research results [29], in order to optimize the experimental conditions, red and blue CDs were synthesized at 160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C, respectively (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1a-S1f, the fluorescence intensity increases and then decreases with increasing reaction temperature, and in particular, at 200 °C, the fluorescence is greatly weakened due to the destruction of chlorophyll, so that the whole emission spectra show bimodal emission. Based on this we chose the reaction temperature of 180 °C. Subsequently, the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity was investigated by reacting at 180 °C for 4 h, 6 h, and 8 h, respectively. When the reaction time reached 6 h, the fluorescence intensity did not increase significantly by continuing to increase the reaction time (Fig. S1f and S1l). Based on the above careful consideration, we chose to conduct the following one-step synthesis of red and blue fluorescent CDs under the reaction conditions of 180 °C and 6 h

In detail, 0.10 g spirulina powder was scattered in 30 mL anhydrous ethanol and the mixture was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated for 6 h at 180 °C (named s-RCDs). After cooling down to room temperature, the products were purified by silica column chromatography with the eluent of ethanol. The solvent then was removed via rotary evaporation and dried under vacuum.

0.10 g spirulina powder was dispersed in 30 mL deionized water and was sonicated for 15 min. The dispersion was moved into a 50 mL Teflon-lined reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h (named s-BCDs). When the reaction was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the purification and drying steps of s-BCDs were consistent with those of s-RCDs.

The morphology and structure were characterized using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X). Raman spectra were gathered using a LabRam HR Evolution spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Tensor 27 ATR-FTIR spectrometer in the KBr tablets. Elemental compositions and valence states of the samples were analyzed by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument (Al Kα monochromatic). The absorption spectra were measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Fluoromax-4). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of all CDs were obtained using an Ultima IV-type multifunctional horizontal X-ray diffractometer. PL lifetimes and absolute quantum yields (QY) were recorded on PluoroLog 3-TCSPC with an integrating sphere. The contact angles (CA) of s-RCDs and s-BCDs were measured by the optical contact angle measuring instrument (OSA 100, LAUDA Scientific, Germany) using the sessile drop method. Cell imaging was conducted using a confocal laser fluorescence microscope (STELLARIS5, Leica, Germany).

The contact angles of the two kinds of CDs were measured using an optical contact angle analyzer to investigate their wettability to water. First, the sample solution was uniformly dripped onto a glass microscope slide to fabricate a wafer, which was then dried in a closed oven at 60 °C for 20 min. Finally, a drop of water (2 μL) was carefully dropped on the wafer using a microsyringe and the morphology of the droplet against the solid surface was captured with a camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL s-RCDs and 100 μL s-BCDs solutions were added into 3800 μL 1,4-dioxane (or ethanol) with different water content with the final volume of 4 mL. The changes in fluorescence intensities of organic solutions with different water content were recorded at 435 nm and 670 nm under excitation of 365 nm. Each group of parallel experiments was conducted three times.

The cytotoxicity of s-RCDs was evaluated by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrabromozole (MTT) assay in human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells. HeLa cells were first seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 104 cells mL−1 and incubated for 24 h at 310 K. Then, the s-RCDs with the concertation from 0 to 100 μg/mL were added to the cultures for 24 h Subsequently, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), and incubated in an incubator for 4 h after the addition of 20 μL MTT solution to each well. Then, the growth medium was removed and 150 μL of DMSO was then added to per well and shaken for 10 min at room temperature. Finally, the cell viability was measured by calculating the absorbance at 490 nm using a microplate reader.

The seeding of the HeLa cells into 96-well plates in a standard medium was carried out for 24 h (310 K with 5 % CO2). Then, we removed the medium and washed the cells with PBS buffer 2∼3 times. Subsequently, the cells were incubated in DMEM combined with 60 μg/mL s-RCDs solution for 4 h The culture medium that had free CDs was subsequently cast off, whereas the washing of the HeLa cells was performed using PBS buffers 2∼3 times. Finally, the fluorescence pictures of cells were attained on a Leica fluorescence microscope with a 405 nm laser.

Spirulina, a widely available and green natural cyanobacteria, was used as raw material, and ethanol and water were used to extract hydrophobic and hydrophilic components to prepare ethanol and aqueous solutions of spirulina, respectively. Both of them showed bright red fluorescence under the 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1). Subsequently, a simple one-step solvothermal treatment was performed to successfully prepare two CDs, hydrophobic CDs with red-emission and hydrophilic CDs with blue-emission, named s-RCDs and s-BCDs, respectively (Fig. 1). The wettability of s-RCDs and s-BCDs to water was further assessed through the contact angle (CA) measurements. It is well known that solid surfaces with contact angle values exceeding 90° are considered to be hydrophobic [33]. The CAs of s-RCDs and s-BCDs supported on the glass slide were determined to be 110° and 19°, respectively (Fig. 1). These results indicate that we have successfully prepared hydrophobic s-RCDs and hydrophilic s-BCDs.

As is known, chlorophyll in plants can emit deep red fluorescence after ethanol extraction, and chlorophyll is hydrophobic. Spirulina is rich in chlorophyll. Organic solvents can extract chlorophyll from spirulina powder, which makes the extracts show bright red fluorescence under ultraviolet light (365 nm). This is confirmed by the fact that the extracts of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone (DMK), toluene (Tol), dichloromethane (DCM), and cyclohexane (CYH) of spirulina powder have similar fluorescence emission to chlorophyll ethanol solution (Fig. S2-S3) [34,35].

In the case of ethanol, the spirulina ethanol extract is green in daylight and emits deep red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (the inset of Fig. 2a ). Spirulina ethanol extract has a broad absorption band extending from the ultraviolet to the deep red (Fig. 2a). It has been reported that the absorption peak recorded at 200∼250 nm indicates the typical absorption behavior of an aromatic π system, and a strong absorption peak and a weak absorption peak are measured in the spectrum of spirulina ethanol extract at around 380∼420 nm and 650∼680 nm, which typically correspond to the Soret band and Q-band of the chlorophyll-derived porphyrins, respectively [36]. When the emission wavelength is 680 nm, the excitation spectrum of spirulina ethanol extract overlaps with the adsorption band (380∼420 nm), suggesting that the fluorescence of spirulina ethanol extract may originate from the chlorophyll molecules. In addition, the FL emission spectra and two-dimensional fluorescence topographical map of spirulina ethanol extract also confirmed this point: highly fluorescent red emission at 680 nm and excitation wavelength-independent emission (Fig. 2b-2c).

Then, s-RCDs were prepared by solvothermal reaction of spirulina ethanol extract at 180 °C for 6 h The optical properties of s-RCDs were studied by UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. The s-RCDs solution appears light green in indoor light and emits bright red fluorescence as expected when excited by 365 nm ultraviolet light (the inset of Fig. 2d). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of s-RCDs is shown in Fig. 2d. There are two strong absorption peaks and a weak absorption peak at 230, 380∼435 and 650∼670 nm, which generally belong to the π-π* transition of the aromatic system, the Soret and Q band of the porphyrin-like structure, respectively. Interestingly, in the FL spectra of s-RCDs, excitation-independent red emission is observed with the maximum intensity at 675 nm (when excited at 430 nm) with a shoulder peak at 726 nm (NIR I region) (Fig. 2e) [37,38]. The 2D emission-excitation mapping of the s-RCDs solution exhibits a typical excitation-independent emission and the fluorescence signal appears as a single mode (Fig. 2f) [13,14]. This characteristic is derived from molecular fluorescence, combined with the fluorescence characteristic of spirulina ethanol extract, indicating that the red fluorescence comes from the chlorophyll-like structure, which once again confirms the fluorescence mechanism proposed by our research group [28].

In addition, red fluorescence can also be obtained by solvothermal reaction of spirulina powder with N,N-dimethylformamide, toluene, acetone, and cyclohexane at 180 °C for 6 h Their fluorescence emission peaks are all located at ∼675 nm, which are independent of excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence emission results are similar to those of s-RCDs (Fig. S4) [16,39]. It is further confirmed that fat-soluble chlorophyll in spirulina can be extracted by organic solvents, and the fluorescence characteristics of chlorophyll-like can be retained even after a solvothermal reaction at 180 °C. This plays an important guideline in the high value-added conversion as well as the application of chlorophyll-containing biomass.

It is noteworthy that chlorophyll is soluble in organic solvents but insoluble in water. The solvent was replaced with water to extract hydrophilic substances from spirulina. In particular, the water dispersion of spirulina powder appears blue in daylight and exhibits red fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1). Spirulina powder aqueous dispersion was subjected to a one-step hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 6 h to generate s-BCDs. The s-BCDs solution is light yellow in the daylight and displays bright blue fluorescence when excited by a 365 nm UV lamp, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 g. Then, the UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of s-BCDs were characterized. As presented in Fig. 2 g, the strong absorption peak at 207 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic C = C, and there are obvious characteristic absorption peaks at 267 nm and 330∼380 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C and the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N of the aromatic system. When the emission wavelength is 458 nm, the characteristic peak of the excitation spectrum coincides with the absorption peak at 330∼380 nm, pointing that the fluorescence comes from the n-π* transition of C = O/C = N in the aromatic structure [40]. In addition, the s-BCDs exhibit an excitation-dependent downconversion PL emission whose strongest emission peak is red-shifted from 407 to 487 nm as the excitation wavelength is increased from 300 to 420 nm, and this FL emission behavior is also reflected in the excitation-emission 2D fluorescence topography of the s-BCDs (Fig. 2h-2i). Generally, this PL property is attributed to the surface passivation which effectively traps the excitons and hinders the non-radiative recombination [41-43].

The same spirulina source was dispersed in different solvents, and the CDs with red fluorescence or blue fluorescence were obtained by simple solvothermal treatment. For example, the CIE color coordinates of s-RCDs and s-BCDs are (0.66, 0.30) and (0.18, 0.17), respectively, which are located in the red light region and the blue light region (Fig. S5). These successfully prepared two types of fluorescent CDs show great potential in the field of optoelectronic devices.

Next, the morphology and structure of s-RCDs and s-BCDs were compared. First of all, the morphology and size of the two kinds of CDs were characterized by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 3a , s-RCDs are spherical-like particles with uniform distribution, good dispersion, and no aggregation, which confirms the formation of CDs. The HRTEM image of s-RCDs showed graphene-like lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) crystal plane diffraction [15]. s-BCDs are quasi-spherical structures without obvious lattice fringes (Fig. 3b), which is in accordance with the reported CPDs [44]. Through statistical calculation, the size distribution range of the two kinds of CDs is 1.0∼3.5 nm, and the average size is 1.99±0.01 nm and 1.84±0.01 nm, respectively (the insets of Fig. 3a-3b). These results show that the quantum size effect should not be the reason for the fluorescence difference. Then, the structures and compositions of s-RCDs and s-BCDs were characterized by XRD, Raman, XPS, and FT-IR spectra. Both s-RCDs and s-BCDs show a broad diffraction peak at around 21° in the XRD pattern, as displayed in Fig. 3c, confirming the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials. The Raman spectra have two peaks at ∼1375 and ∼1575 cm−1, corresponding to the D band (representing the carbon associated with defects and disorder) and the G band (representing the in-plane stretching vibration of the sp2 domains). The strength ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) represents the degree of graphitization. The ID/IG of s-RCDs and s-BCDs are 0.94 and 1.07, respectively, which indicates that the graphitization degree of s-RCDs is higher than that of s-BCDs (Fig. 3d) [45].

The chemical compositions of the surfaces of s-RCDs and s-BCDs were further investigated utilizing XPS. From Fig. 3e, C 1 s (284 eV), N 1 s (399 eV), and O 1 s (531 eV) can be mainly identified, illustrating that the surfaces of the two prepared CDs are enriched with nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups, and their detailed elemental ratios are shown in Table S1, with similar proportions of the various elements on the surfaces of s-RCDs and s-BCDs. As seen in Fig. S6, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of s-RCDs can be divided into three peaks, namely CC/C = C (284.8 eV), CO/CN (286.4 eV), C = O/C = N (288.8 eV) [46]. The three peaks in the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum can be resolved to pyridine N (398.4 eV), pyrrole N (399.8 eV), and graphite N (401.1 eV), respectively [47]. The O 1 s spectrum has two peaks at 530.76 eV and 531.9 eV, corresponding to C = O (531.1 eV) and CO (532.6 eV) [48]. s-BCDs and s-RCDs have similar surface compositions but different contents. In particular, as shown in Table S2, the ratio of C = O/C = N on the surface of s-BCDs is higher than that of s-RCDs, which is most likely the reason for the difference in fluorescence between the two. The FT-IR spectra of s-RCDs and s-BCDs are further compared (Fig. 3f). The characteristic peaks of s-RCDs and s-BCDs at ∼3430 cm−1, 2850∼2930 cm−1, ∼1400 cm−1, and 900∼500 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH/-NH, the stretching vibration of -CH2, the bending vibration of CN and the bending vibration of CH of aromatic rings, respectively [49]. In particular, the CN asymmetric stretching vibration at 1120 cm−1, the C = N stretching vibration at 1540∼1580 cm−1, and the amide bond vibration absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 of s-BCDs are stronger than those in s-RCDs, while the CO-C bond strength of s-RCDs is higher than that of s-BCDs. The above results of FT-IR are consistent with XPS and UV–vis absorption spectra.

The optical properties of red fluorescent substance extracted from spirulina powder by ethanol, s-RCDs, and chlorophyll are almost the same, indicating that the chlorophyll in spirulina extracted by ethanol is limited in the carbon skeleton after solvothermal reaction and still retains red light emission. It once again validates the red fluorescence mechanism of biomass-derived CDs proposed by our team. According to the literature, spirulina not only contains rich chlorophyll but also contains unique phycocyanin. Phycocyanin is a blue powder, non-toxic, soluble in water, but insoluble in ethanol and oils. Phycocyanin from spirulina can be extracted by ultrasonic method with water as the solvent, and the extract shows blue in sunlight and bright red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp [50]. Then, we compared the fluorescence properties of spirulina in mixed solutions of ethanol and water at different ratios (%, v/v). As the amount of ethanol in the solution decreases, the color of the spirulina solution gradually changes from green to cyan and finally to blue under sunlight. Correspondingly, under the 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence color gradually changes from bright deep red to light and then to red fluorescence. The detailed fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. S7, and the position of the optimal fluorescence emission peak gradually shifts from 680 nm to a short wave band. When the content of ethanol is 25 %, the FL spectra show a double peak emission, while when the solvent is water, the optimal fluorescence emission peak is located at 650 nm which is blue-shifted by 30 nm.

Then, we compared the UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectra of the aqueous extract of spirulina and the aqueous solution of pure phycocyanin. The UV–vis absorption spectra of both cover almost the entire UV-visible region, and both show characteristic absorption peaks near the wavelengths of 275 nm, 350∼400 nm, and 620 nm (Fig. 4a -4b). In addition, the FL emission spectra of the two are very similar, showing obvious excitation-independent photoluminescence, with the optimal emission peak at 650 nm and a shoulder peak located at 700 nm (Fig. 4d-4e) [51,52]. Combined with the structural formulas of phycocyanin and chlorophyll, we found that phycocyanin is an open-chain tetrapyrrole structure, while chlorophyll is composed of a large porphyrin ring composed of tetrapyrrole. Although both are large conjugated systems, the conjugation degree of chlorophyll is higher than that of phycocyanin (the insets of Fig. S3 and Fig. 4b). This explains why both spirulina ethanol solution and spirulina aqueous solution emit red fluorescence, but the position of the optimal fluorescence emission peak is different by 30 nm. In short, chlorophyll in spirulina can be extracted from ethanol and other organic solvents, while phycocyanin can be extracted from water by ultrasound method. However, the thermal stability of phycocyanin is inferior, it can remain stable below 40 °C, but when the temperature rises to 45 °C, the pigment will decompose and its fluorescence intensity will be greatly weakened. To further verify the changes of phycocyanin in the synthesis of s-BCDs, phycocyanin was directly selected as the research object, and its optical changes were characterized after the hydrothermal reaction at 100 °C for 3 h As presented in Fig. 4c, the characteristic absorption peak of phycocyanin at 620 nm completely disappeared after the hydrothermal reaction, indicating that phycocyanin was decomposed. Accordingly, the emission spectra show wavelength-dependent emission and weak fluorescence intensity, and the instrument noise peak appears (Fig. 4f).

In combination with the results of the above analyses, we proposed a possible reaction mechanism (Fig. 5 ). The abundant chlorophyll in spirulina is insoluble in water but can be extracted with organic solvents such as ethanol, and the extract emits deep red fluorescence. After the solvothermal reaction, the chlorophyll-like structure is confined in the carbon dots skeleton and still retains red light emission. The unique phycocyanin in spirulina can be extracted from water by ultrasound, and the extract emits red fluorescence. However, phycocyanin has poor thermal stability and decomposes at 45 °C. Therefore, in the early stage of the hydrothermal reaction, it decomposes into simple small molecules, and then small molecules dehydrate, polymerize, and carbonize to CDs in the hydrothermal reaction. As a consequence, the degree of graphitization of s-BCDs is lower than that of s-RCDs, but the surface functional groups are richer, which is confirmed by Raman and FT-IR analysis results.

Based on the mechanism analysis of s-RCDs and s-BCDs, we discovered that s-RCDs still retained chlorophyll properties, such as being soluble in organic solvents such as ethanol and insoluble in water, while s-BCDs were soluble in hydrophilic solvents. Therefore, we attempted to develop a ratiometric sensor based on solubility detection of s-RCDs and s-BCDs. An equal volume of s-RCDs ethanol solution and s-BCDs aqueous solution were mixed, and stratification appeared before shaking, as displayed in Fig. S8a. The upper layer was s-RCDs with red fluorescence, and the lower layer was s-BCDs with blue fluorescence. After standing for 30 days, the two phases were homogeneous, so, the different densities of s-RCDs and s-BCDs may be the cause of the stratification. After shaking well, the two phases were mixed, and the overall fluorescence was blue (Fig. S8b). After 30 days, the uniform blue fluorescence was still displayed, which may be due to the hydrophobic aggregation of s-RCDs.

Consequently, based on the uniform mixing of s-RCDs and s-BCDs (Fig. S8b), the same amount of different solvents was added: water, anhydrous ethanol, n-butanol, N,N-dimethylformamide, cyclohexane, toluene, dichloromethane, and chloroform. Due to the hydrophobicity of s-RCDs, the whole sample showed blue fluorescence after adding water (Fig. 6a(I) ). However, with the addition of ethanol solvent, the whole solution showed pink fluorescence and restored the red fluorescence of s-RCDs (Fig. 6a(II)). We speculate that with the decrease of water content in the solution, the effect of solvent on s-RCDs is weakened, so the original red fluorescence of s-RCDs is restored. The phenomena of adding n-butanol and N,N-dimethylformamide were the same as that of ethanol, with the homogeneous solution and overall pink fluorescence (Fig. 6a(III-IV)) [53]. Since cyclohexane and toluene are insoluble in water and less dense than water, stratification occurs. Red fluorescence existed in the upper organic solvent layer, and blue fluorescence appeared in the lower water layer (Fig. 6a(V-VI)) [32]. Dichloromethane and chloroform, on the other hand, are denser than water, so the fluorescence stratification is the opposite, with blue fluorescence in the upper layer and red fluorescence in the lower layer (Fig. 6a(VII-VIII)). After 30 days of storage, the above organic solvent-water systems can still observe obvious fluorescence behavior, indicating that the hydrophobic-hydrophilic sensing based on s-RCDs and s-BCDs has good photostability. The experimental results confirm our speculation that s-RCDs and s-BCDs inherit some properties of the precursors. This study provides a new perspective on the selection of precursors for synthesizing CDs with specific properties.

Because of the different wettability of s-RCDs and s-BCDs to water, it is attempted to design them as a ratiometric sensor to detect water content. In order to verify the reliability of the sensor for the detection of water content, it was used for the detection of water content in 1,4-dioxane. As displayed in the inset of Fig. 6b, the fluorescence color observed by the naked eye gradually changed from red to blue with increasing water content under 365 nm UV light. The FL emission at 435 nm and 670 nm both changed with water content increasing from 0 to 100 % (Fig. 6b). So, the fluorescence intensity ratio at 435 nm and 670 nm (I435/I670) was utilized for the determination of water content. Fig. 6c shows the linear response to water content from 60 % to 100 %, corresponding to the equation y = 0.523x+28.956 (R2=0.989). In addition, the results of water content measurements in ethanol are plotted in Fig. S9 and are similar to the water response in 1,4-dioxane: I435/I670 was linearly related to the water content increases from 60 % to 100 %. Therefore, this ratiometric sensor based on s-RCDs and s-BCDs provides a fast and convenient method for the determination of water content in organic solvents, which can be extended to applications in harsh environments.

In addition, the long-term stability of spirulina ethanol extract, spirulina water extract, s-RCDs, and s-BCDs solutions under room temperature storage was also studied. As shown in the results of Fig. S10a-S10b, the fluorescence intensity of the spirulina extracts was greatly weakened after 180 days of storage. In particular, the fluorescence of the spirulina aqueous solution almost completely disappeared. After solvothermal treatment, the fluorescence intensity of s-RCDs did not decrease significantly after 180 days (Fig. S10c). It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of s-BCDs was strengthened after being placed for 180 days, which is similar to the phenomenon of moss-derived CDs prepared by our research group and can be attributed to the crosslink-enhanced emission effect (Fig. S10d) [54]. The above results show that these two kinds of CDs have good photostability, which gives them broad application prospects in the field of biological imaging.

In addition to photostability, the lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield of CDs are also important features for evaluating biological imaging capabilities. Firstly, the time-resolved PL decay curves of s-RCDs and s-BCDs measured according to time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) were used to study the kinetics of electron deactivation (transfer from an excited state to the ground state) via radiative and nonradiative pathways [55]. Fig. S11a and S11b show fluorescence decay curves and their exponential fitting results. s-RCDs can be fitted to a single exponential model with a fluorescence lifetime of 6.0 ns, while S-BCDs can be fitted to a double exponential curve with an average fluorescence lifetime of 3.4 ns. Then, the absolute quantum yields (QY) of s-RCDs and s-BCDs were measured using a quantum efficiency measurement system with a calibrated integrating sphere. As shown in Fig. S11c, the QY of s-RCDs is 9.8 % at the optimal excitation wavelength of 411 nm. At the optimal excitation wavelength of 356 nm, the QY of s-BCDs is 4.8 % (Fig. S11d).

Based on the above excellent optical properties, we tried to develop s-RCDs in the NIR I region into excellent biological imaging agents. To begin with, the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of s-RCDs to HeLa cells were carefully evaluated by MTT assay [56]. To our delight, when incubated with s-RCDs at different concentrations (0∼100 μg/mL) for 24 h, the cell viability of HeLa cells remained above 95 % (Fig. S12). Given the low cytotoxicity, we evaluated the fluorescence imaging capability of s-RCDs for HeLa. HeLa cells were incubated with s-RCDs (60 μg mL−1) at 37 °C for 4 h, and then confocal fluorescence imaging was performed. As expected, these HeLa cells presented bright red fluorescence when excited at a wavelength of 405 nm, as shown in Fig. 7 , indicating that s-RCDs were able to cross the cell membrane, enter the cytoplasm and nucleus, and successfully label HeLa cells [57]. The results show that s-RCDs can be safely and efficiently used as fluorescent probes in the field of bioimaging.

In conclusion, we developed a simple “performance-inheritance” strategy for the first synthesis of hydrophobic s-RCDs with red fluorescence and hydrophilic s-BCDs with blue fluorescence using spirulina as a precursor. The red fluorescent s-RCDs were solvothermally synthesized by extracting fat-soluble chlorophyll from spirulina using organic solvents, and the blue fluorescent s-BCDs were prepared by hydrothermal reaction with water as a solvent to extract water-soluble phycocyanin. It is further speculated that the red fluorescence of s-RCDs originates from the chlorophyll-like structure, while the blue fluorescence of s-BCDs comes from the surface state defects. In particular, sensing detection based on s-RCDs and s-BCDs shows good application prospects for determining water content in organic solutions. In addition, s-RCDs with NIR fluorescence have good biocompatibility and high stability and can be used as fluorescent probes for live cell imaging. This “performance-inheritance” strategy provides a new perspective for designing the preparation of CDs with specific properties.

Fu Qin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pinyi He: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinyu Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Guohui Yang: Methodology, Investigation. Xu Yu: Methodology, Investigation. Yongkang Yao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lili Ren: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.105274.
